graphical-sequence-test|Havel-Hakimi Graphical Sequence Test|to test if a non-increasing degree sequence is realisable by a simple graph: remove the largest degree d, subtract 1 from the next d entries, re-sort, and repeat; the sequence is graphical iff this reduces to all zeros without producing a negative value

havel-hakimi-test|Havel-Hakimi Test for Graphic Sequences|Sort degrees non-increasing; remove the largest degree d_1 and subtract 1 from the next d_1 entries; re-sort and repeat. All zeros => graphic; a negative entry => not graphic.
chromatic-number-cycle|Chromatic Number of a Cycle C_n|A cycle on n vertices needs 2 colours when n is even and 3 colours when n is odd; equivalently chi(C_n) = n - 2*floor(n/2) + 2.
clique-chromatic-bound|Clique Lower Bound on Chromatic Number|The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique; if G contains a K_k then at least k colours are needed.
bipartite-iff-no-odd-cycle|Bipartite Characterisation (no odd cycle)|A graph is bipartite (vertices split into two sides with all edges crossing) exactly when it contains no cycle of odd length.
chromatic-bipartite|Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph|Any bipartite graph that has at least one edge can be properly coloured with exactly 2 colours.
four-color-theorem|Four Colour Theorem|Every planar graph can be properly vertex-coloured using at most 4 colours.
chromatic-clique-lower-bound|Clique Lower Bound for Chromatic Number|The chromatic number is at least the size of the largest clique, since every vertex of a clique must get a different colour.
vizing-theorem|Vizing's Theorem (Edge Chromatic Number)|For any simple graph, the minimum number of colours to edge-colour it (the chromatic index) is either the maximum degree or one more than it.

greedy-coloring-bound|Greedy (First-Fit) Colouring Bound|Colouring vertices one by one and always choosing the smallest colour not used by already-coloured neighbours produces a proper colouring using at most Delta+1 colours.

chromatic-independent-set-bound|Independent Set Lower Bound from Chromatic Number|In any proper colouring with chi colours the n vertices split into chi colour classes (each independent), so the largest one has at least n/chi vertices.

chromatic-edge-lower-bound|Edge Lower Bound from Chromatic Number|A graph with chromatic number k must have at least one edge between every pair of colour classes, so it has at least C(k,2) = k(k-1)/2 edges - as many as the complete graph K_k.
chromatic-number-bipartite|Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph|chi(G) = 2 for a bipartite graph that has at least one edge; chi = 1 if it has no edges.
hypercube-properties|Hypercube Q_n: Chromatic Number and Diameter|The n-dimensional hypercube Q_n is bipartite so chi(Q_n) = 2, and its diameter equals n (the maximum Hamming distance).
odd-cycle-forces-three-colours|Odd Cycle Forces At Least Three Colours|If a graph contains a cycle of odd length it is not bipartite, so it needs at least 3 colours.
adjacency-matrix-def|Adjacency Matrix (definition)|A[i][j] = 1 if there is an edge from vertex i to vertex j, else 0
max-edges-k-components|Maximum Edges in a Graph with n Vertices and k Components|max edges = C(n-k+1, 2) = (n-k)(n-k+1)/2
tree-edges|Edges in a Tree|a tree on m vertices has exactly m - 1 edges
forest-edges|Edges in a Forest|a forest with n vertices and p tree-components has n - p edges
cycle-rank|Cycle Rank (Circuit Rank / First Betti Number)|number of independent cycles = E - V + (number of components)
regular-graph-edges|Edges in a d-Regular Graph|a d-regular graph on n vertices has nd/2 edges

tree-mis-greedy-leaf|Greedy Maximum Independent Set on a Tree (Leaf Rule)|On a tree, a maximum independent set is found greedily by repeatedly selecting a leaf (degree <= 1), adding it to I when none of its neighbours is already in I, then deleting it; runs in O(n).

articulation-point-definition|Articulation Point (Cut Vertex)|A vertex v is an articulation point (cut vertex) if removing v (and its incident edges) increases the number of connected components of the graph.

edge-connectivity-min-degree|Edge Connectivity Bounded by Minimum Degree|The edge connectivity lambda(G) (min-cut cardinality) is at most the minimum degree delta(G), because the edges at any vertex form a cut isolating it; hence a min-cut of size k forces every vertex degree to be at least k.
components-after-vertex-removal|Components After Removing One Vertex|k - 1 <= components(G - v) <= n - 1
edges-union-connected-graphs|Edge Count of a Graph Union (Inclusion-Exclusion on Edges)||E_1 union E_2| = |E_1| + |E_2| - |E_1 intersect E_2|
vertex-cover-independent-complement|Gallai Identity (Vertex Cover and Independent Set)|alpha(G) + tau(G) = |V|  (max independent set + min vertex cover = number of vertices)
connected-components-count|Counting Connected Components by Grouping Vertices|components(G) = number of isolated vertices + number of larger connected pieces
eulerian-circuit-condition|Eulerian Circuit Condition|A connected graph has an Eulerian circuit iff every vertex has even degree
complement-degree|Vertex Degree in the Complement Graph|deg in complement = (n-1) - deg in original
forest-edge-count|Edges in a Forest|a forest with n vertices and k components (trees) has n - k edges
max-edges-bipartite|Maximum Edges in a Bipartite Graph|a bipartite graph on v vertices has at most floor(v^2 / 4) edges, achieved by the balanced complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} with m + n = v

grid-king-edges|Edges in an m x n King-Move Grid Graph|for an m x n grid where cells are adjacent when both coordinate differences are at most 1 (king moves), the edges are horizontal m(n-1) + vertical n(m-1) + two diagonal families 2(m-1)(n-1)
line-graph-incidence|Line Graph Adjacency Rule|in the line graph L(G), each edge of G becomes a vertex, and two such vertices are adjacent exactly when the original edges share an endpoint
bridge-cycle-criterion|Bridge-Cycle Criterion|an edge is a bridge (cut edge) if and only if it does not lie on any cycle of the graph
hamiltonian-cycles-complete|Number of Hamiltonian Cycles in a Complete Graph K_n|Number of distinct Hamiltonian cycles in K_n = (n-1)!/2

mst-uniqueness-criterion|Uniqueness Criterion for the Minimum Spanning Tree|A connected weighted graph has a unique MST iff every cut of the graph has a unique minimum-weight edge crossing it; all-distinct edge weights is a sufficient special case.
graph-square-diameter|Diameter of the Graph Square G^2|If G^2 joins every pair of vertices within distance 2 of each other in G (adjacency from M OR M^2), then diam(G^2) = ceil(diam(G)/2).

max-edges-disconnected-graph|Maximum Edges in a Disconnected Simple Graph|A disconnected simple graph on n vertices has at most C(n-1, 2) edges, achieved by a complete graph K_(n-1) plus one isolated vertex.
closed-walks-trace-power|Closed Walks via Trace of a Matrix Power|number of closed walks of length k = trace(A^k) = sum_i (A^k)_{ii}
adjacency-square-degree|Diagonal of A^2 Gives Vertex Degrees|(A^2)_{ii} = deg(i); (A^2)_{ij} = number of common neighbours of i and j (i != j)
cayley-spanning-trees|Cayley's Formula (Spanning Trees of K_n)|number of spanning trees of K_n = n^(n-2)
fundamental-cycle-swap|Spanning-Tree Edge Swap on a Fundamental Cycle|T' = T - e + f  =>  w(T') - w(T) = w(f) - w(e)

petersen-graph-invariants|Standard Invariants of the Petersen Graph|Petersen graph: 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, girth 5, chromatic number 3, independence number 4, has a Hamiltonian path but no Hamiltonian cycle
perfect-matchings-complete-graph|Number of Perfect Matchings of K_{2m}|number of perfect matchings of K_{2m} = (2m)!/(2^m * m!) = (2m-1)!!

eulers-formula-planar|Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs|for a connected planar graph drawn in the plane, vertices minus edges plus faces equals 2 (the faces include the unbounded outer face)

euler-formula-planar|Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs|V - E + F = 2

conjunction-elimination|Conjunction Elimination (Simplification)|from (A and B), conclude either conjunct (e.g. B)
negation-of-quantifiers|Negation of Quantifiers (De Morgan for Quantifiers)|~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x) and ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
negation-of-conjunction-implication|Negated Conjunction as Implication|~(A and B) = (A -> ~B)
material-implication|Material Implication|P -> Q = ~P or Q
all-a-are-b|Categorical "All A are B"|"All A are B" = forall x (A(x) -> B(x))
tautology-definition|Tautology / Valid wff|A formula is a tautology (valid) iff it is true under every interpretation/model.
quantifier-order-noncommute|Quantifier Order (forall-exists vs exists-forall)|exists y forall x R implies forall x exists y R, but NOT conversely
exists-distributes-over-or|Existential Distributes over Disjunction|exists x (P or Q) = (exists x P) or (exists x Q)
forall-not-distribute-over-or|Universal Does NOT Distribute over Disjunction|forall x (P or Q) does NOT equal (forall x P) or (forall x Q)
existential-instantiation-weakening|Existential Weakening from a Universal (non-empty domain)|On a non-empty domain, forall x exists y R(x,y) implies exists x exists y R(x,y)
wff-validity-definition|Validity of a Well-Formed Formula|F is valid (a tautology) iff value(F, I) = True for every interpretation I
material-implication-law|Material Implication Law|A -> B == not A or B
contrapositive-law|Contrapositive Law|A -> B == not B -> not A
implication-tautology-forces-consequent|Tautological Implication Forces Its Consequent|If X -> Y is a tautology and X is True under I, then Y is True under I
conjunction-implies-disjunction|Conjunction Implies Shared-Term Disjunction|(a and b) -> (b or c) is a tautology
universal-instantiation|Universal Instantiation|from for-all x F(x) infer F(a) for any constant a
resolution-rule|Resolution Rule|from (X or L) and (Y or not L) infer the resolvent (X or Y); the empty clause means inconsistency
disjunctive-syllogism|Disjunctive Syllogism|from P or Q and not P conclude Q
prop-equivalence-laws|Propositional Equivalence Laws (distributive, complement, identity)|Distributive: p ^ (q v r) = (p ^ q) v (p ^ r); Complement: p ^ ~p = F; Identity: p v F = p

pcnf-canonical-form|Principal (Canonical) Conjunctive Normal Form via Maxterms|PCNF = AND of maxterms over all 0-rows; in a 0-row a variable appears plain if its value is 0 and negated if its value is 1
prop-tautology-test|Tautology Test (truth-table validity)|A formula is a tautology iff it is true under every assignment; to show it is NOT a tautology, exhibit one row that makes it false
implication-as-disjunction|Implication as Disjunction|p -> q == ~p \/ q
negation-laws|Negation / Excluded-Middle and Domination Laws|p \/ ~p == T ; p /\ ~p == F ; T \/ p == T ; F /\ p == F
implication-truth-value|Truth Value of an Implication|p -> q is False only when p is True and q is False; True in all other cases
only-if-translation|Translation of 'Only If'|P only if Q == P -> Q
converse-of-implication|Converse of an Implication|converse(P -> Q) == Q -> P
unless-translation|Translation of 'Unless'|P unless Z == ~Z -> P
exportation-law|Exportation Law|(A /\ B) -> C == A -> (B -> C)
idempotent-law|Idempotent Laws|p \/ p == p ; p /\ p == p
demorgan-laws|De Morgan's Laws|~(p \/ q) == ~p /\ ~q ; ~(p /\ q) == ~p \/ ~q
logical-implication-truth|Truth Condition of Material Implication|An implication X -> Y is false only when X is true and Y is false; in every other case it is true. Equivalently X -> Y equals ~X v Y.

logical-equivalence-truth-table|Logical Equivalence via Truth Tables|Two formulas are logically equivalent when they have identical truth values in every row of the truth table; a single differing row disproves equivalence.
demorgan-law|De Morgan's Laws|Negating a conjunction flips it to a disjunction of negations, and vice versa. Pushing a negation inward swaps AND with OR and negates each part.

minterm-sum-truth-vector|Sum-of-Minterms Truth Vector|A function written as an OR of minterms is true exactly on the rows whose minterms appear in the sum; listing those rows gives the function's truth vector directly.
de-morgans-law|De Morgan's Laws|not(A and B) = (not A) or (not B); not(A or B) = (not A) and (not B)
only-if-implication|'Only If' as Implication|'A only if B' means A -> B (B is necessary for A)
when-implication|'When' Clause as Implication|'Y when X' means X -> Y (the 'when' clause is the antecedent)
distributive-law|Distributive Law (and over or)|A and (B or C) == (A and B) or (A and C)
self-implication-tautology|Self-Implication Tautology|X -> X is always True for any formula X
context-implication-simplify|Antecedent-Context Simplification of an Implication|If the antecedent A guarantees Q, then A -> (Q -> R) is equivalent to A -> R
vacuous-truth|Vacuous Truth (False Antecedent)|An implication with a False antecedent is automatically True

argument-validity-tautology|Validity of a Propositional Argument (Tautology Test)|An argument with premises P_1,...,P_n and conclusion C is valid iff the conditional (P_1 /\ ... /\ P_n) -> C is true under every truth assignment; equivalently, there is no assignment making all premises true and C false.

commutative-binary-op-count|Number of Commutative Binary Operations on an n-Element Set|the number of commutative binary operations on a set of n elements is n^(n(n+1)/2), since the symmetric operation table has n(n+1)/2 freely chosen cells (diagonal plus upper triangle) each filled with one of n values

associative-binary-operation|Associativity of a Binary Operation|a binary operation * is associative when regrouping the operands does not change the result, i.e. (a*b)*c equals a*(b*c) for all elements a, b, c
commutative-property|Commutative Property of a Binary Operation|a * b = b * a for all a, b
associative-property|Associative Property of a Binary Operation|(a * b) * c = a * (b * c) for all a, b, c
identity-element-binary-op|Identity Element of a Binary Operation|z is an identity if f(a, z) = a = f(z, a) for every a
xor-operator|XOR (Exclusive OR) Operator|p XOR q = 1 when p and q differ, equal to (p + q) mod 2
nand-operator|NAND Operator (X' + Y')|X NAND Y = X' + Y' = complement of (X AND Y)

fol-prenex-bounded-model|Bounded Model Size from Existential Witnesses (Prenex FOL)|A prenex first-order sentence with no function symbols and k essential existential witnesses has a model of size at most k (universals can reuse the witness elements).
fol-divisibility-prime-def|Divisor Characterization of a Prime|An integer n is prime exactly when its only divisors are 1 and n itself (1 satisfies the clause vacuously).

fol-quantifier-distribution|Quantifier Distribution over Connectives (constant W)|When W has no free x: forall distributes over v as forall x (p(x) v W) == (forall x p(x)) v W; exists distributes over ^ as exists x (p(x) ^ W) == (exists x p(x)) ^ W; and in an implication the antecedent quantifier flips, so forall x (p(x) -> W) == (exists x p(x)) -> W.
fol-implication-validity|Semantic Implication Between FOL Statements|Statement R implies statement C when every interpretation (model) that makes R true also makes C true.
exclusive-or-definition|Exclusive-Or Definition|p XOR q is true exactly when p and q differ; it equals (not p and q) or (p and not q) and is the negation of the biconditional
logical-implication-entailment|Logical Implication (Entailment)|F logically implies E iff E is true in every assignment where F is true
vacuous-implication|Vacuous Truth of Implication|an implication with a false antecedent is automatically true (false -> anything = true)
countable-subset-closure|Countability is Closed Under Subsets and Countable Unions|any subset of a countable set is countable; a countable union of countable sets is countable

cantor-power-set-uncountable|Cantor: Functions N -> {0,1} (Power Set of N) are Uncountable|the set of functions from N to {0,1} (equivalently the power set of N, or infinite binary strings) is uncountable
onto-implies-one-to-one-finite|On a Finite Set, Onto Self-Map Implies One-to-One|for a finite set A, a surjective function f: A -> A is automatically injective (and vice versa), so it is a bijection
image-of-preimage-subset|Image of a Preimage is Contained in the Set|f[ f^{-1}[D] ] subseteq D  (with equality on D intersect range(f))
unique-factorization-encoding|Unique Factorization (Prime-Power / Godel Encoding)|N = prod p_i^{e_i} uniquely encodes the exponent list (e_1, e_2, ...)
composition-onto-forces-outer-onto|Onto Composition Forces the Outer Function Onto|if h = f o g is onto then f is onto; range(f o g) subseteq range(f)
double-counting-incidences|Double Counting of Element-Set Incidences|sum_i f(i) = sum_j |X_j| = number of (i, j) with i in X_j
count-boolean-functions|Number of Boolean Functions of n Variables|number of Boolean functions of n variables = 2^(2^n)
surjective-functions-count|Number of Onto (Surjective) Functions|Onto functions from an m-set onto a k-set = sum_{i=0}^{k} (-1)^i C(k,i) (k-i)^m = k! * S(m,k)
preimage-intersection-distributes|Preimage Distributes over Intersection|f^(-1)(S intersect T) = f^(-1)(S) intersect f^(-1)(T) for every function f
involution-function|Involution (Self-Inverse Function)|f(f(i)) = i means f is its own inverse: a bijection made of 2-cycles and fixed points
function-composition|Function Composition|(g o h)(x) = g(h(x)) — substitute h(x) into g wherever its variable appears

group-axioms|Group Axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse)|A set G with operation * is a group if it is closed, associative, has an identity e, and every element has an inverse; a monoid satisfies the first three, a semigroup the first two.
abelian-definition|Abelian (Commutative) Group Condition|A group is abelian when its operation commutes for every pair of elements.
subgroup-test|One-Step Subgroup Test|A non-empty subset H of a group G is a subgroup if and only if for all a, b in H, the element a * b^(-1) is also in H.
abelian-product-power|Square of a Product in an Abelian Group|In a group, (a*b)^2 equals a^2 * b^2 exactly when a and b commute; thus this identity holds for all pairs precisely in an abelian group.
probability-equally-likely|Probability with Equally Likely Outcomes|P(event) = (number of favorable outcomes) / (total number of equally likely outcomes)

bijection-equal-cardinality|Bijection Exists If and Only If Equal Cardinality (Finite Sets)|two finite sets admit a bijection exactly when they have the same number of elements; a bijection is also a surjection and an injection

function-image-equivalence-classes|Image Size Bounded by Forced-Equality Classes|if functional rules force f to take equal values on linked inputs, the number of distinct values f can take is at most the number of equivalence classes those links create

kernel-quotient-bijection|Canonical Bijection from the Quotient by f's Kernel onto its Image|for the relation a~b iff f(a)=f(b), the induced map F([x]) = f(x) is well-defined and one-to-one; if f is onto, F is an onto bijection onto B

composition-onto-implies-outer-onto|Surjectivity of a Composition Implies the Outer Map is Surjective|if the composition h = g . f is onto its codomain C, then the outer function g must be onto C, because range(g . f) is always a subset of range(g); the inner map f need not be onto

shannon-expansion|Shannon's Expansion of a Boolean Function|any boolean function can be expanded about a variable x_i as f = x_i' f(...,x_i=0,...) + x_i f(...,x_i=1,...); the literals x_i' and x_i act as selectors that pick the correct cofactor

group-cancellation-law|Cancellation Laws in a Group|in any group you may cancel a common factor: left cancellation says a*x = a*y implies x = y, and right cancellation says x*a = y*a implies x = y, because every element has an inverse

algebraic-structure-hierarchy|Hierarchy of Algebraic Structures|the standard algebraic structures form a ladder where each adds one axiom: a semigroup has an associative operation, a monoid adds an identity, a group adds inverses, and an abelian group adds commutativity

involution-equiv-order-two|Self-Inverse Element (Involution)|an element a of a group is its own inverse exactly when a^2 = e; such an element has order 1 (the identity) or order 2; non-self-inverse elements pair up as {a, a^{-1}}, which is the basis of the even-order counting argument

semigroup-associativity|Semigroup Associativity|a semigroup is a set with a binary operation * that is associative, meaning regrouping does not change the result; no identity, inverses, or commutativity may be assumed

square-injective-semigroup|Square-Cancellation in a Semigroup|in a semigroup where the squaring map is injective, two elements with equal squares must be equal; to prove p = q it suffices to show p*p = q*q (using associativity to compute the squares)

coset-size-equals-subgroup|Cosets Have the Size of the Subgroup|every left coset aH = {a*h : h in H} has exactly as many elements as H, because left-multiplication by a (the map h -> a*h) is a bijection from H onto aH

cosets-partition-group|Cosets Partition the Group (Equal or Disjoint)|any two left cosets of a subgroup are either identical or completely disjoint, and together the distinct cosets cover the whole group, so they partition G into blocks
lagrange-theorem|Lagrange's Theorem|for a finite group G and subgroup H, the order of H divides the order of G; specifically |G| equals the index [G:H] (number of distinct cosets) times |H|

monoid-structure|Monoid (Identity but Possibly No Inverses)|a monoid is a set with an associative operation and a two-sided identity element; it becomes a group only if, in addition, every element has an inverse
order-of-element|Order of an Element|order(x) = smallest positive integer k with x^k = e
cyclic-generator-order|Generators of a Cyclic Group|an element generates a finite cyclic group exactly when its order equals the group's order
largest-proper-divisor|Largest Proper Subgroup Size|the largest possible proper subgroup order is |G| divided by the smallest prime factor of |G|
involution-commute|Commuting Involutions Generate the Klein Four-Group|two order-2 elements whose product also has order 2 commute, generating a group of at most 4 elements
equivalence-relation|Equivalence Relation|a relation is an equivalence relation if it is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive

lattice-complement|Complement of an Element in a Bounded Lattice|in a bounded lattice with top I and bottom O, an element x is a complement of a when their join is the top (a OR x = I) and their meet is the bottom (a AND x = O); an element can have zero, one, or several complements

divisor-lattice|Divisibility Lattice (GCD as Meet, LCM as Join)|under the divisibility order (a <= b iff a divides b) the meet of two numbers is their gcd and the join is their lcm; on the set of divisors of a fixed n this is a closed, distributive lattice with bottom 1 and top n

lattice-definition|Lattice (Join and Meet)|a poset is a lattice if every pair of elements x, y has a unique least upper bound x join y (the join) and a unique greatest lower bound x meet y (the meet); a finite lattice always has a top 1 and a bottom 0

complement-in-lattice|Complement in a Bounded Lattice|in a bounded lattice with top 1 and bottom 0, an element y is a complement of x when their join is the top and their meet is the bottom; an element may have zero, one, or several complements

powerset-lattice|Powerset Lattice (2^S, subset)|the set of all subsets of S ordered by inclusion forms a lattice in which the join of two subsets is their union and the meet is their intersection; it is a Boolean lattice with 2^|S| elements, bottom empty set and top S

distributive-lattice|Distributive Lattice Law|a lattice is distributive when meet distributes over join (equivalently join over meet) for all elements; the diamond M3 and the pentagon N5 are the two smallest lattices that violate this, and any non-distributive lattice contains one of them

boolean-algebra|Boolean Algebra (Complemented Distributive Lattice)|a Boolean algebra is a bounded lattice that is both distributive and complemented (every element has a complement, which is then unique); failing distributivity or complementation disqualifies it
distributive-lattice-law|Distributive Law for Lattices|a lattice is distributive when x v (y ^ z) = (x v y) ^ (x v z) holds for all x, y, z (equivalently the dual x ^ (y v z) = (x ^ y) v (x ^ z))
gcd-lcm-product|Product of GCD and LCM|lcm(a, b) * gcd(a, b) = a * b for positive integers a, b
fundamental-thm-arithmetic|Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic|every integer > 1 factors uniquely into a product of prime powers
gcd-lcm-prime-exponents|GCD and LCM via Prime Exponents|per prime, gcd takes the minimum exponent and lcm takes the maximum exponent
composition-surjective-rule|Surjectivity of a Composition|if f o g is onto then the outer function f is onto, but the inner function g need not be onto or one-to-one
mirsky-theorem|Mirsky's Theorem|the minimum number of antichains needed to partition a poset equals the length of its longest chain
partial-order-axioms|Partial Order Axioms|a relation is a partial order if it is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
hasse-cover-relation|Hasse Diagram Cover Relation|draw an edge between x and y only when x < y with no element strictly between them (a cover)
linear-extension-poset|Linear Extensions of a Poset|a total order containing a partial order is a linear extension; counting them means counting orderings consistent with all given relations
